note:
上个月，总裁唐纳德·特朗普和他的盟友试图让人怀疑总统当选人拜登＆＃39; S在总统选举中获胜。但是，他们未能提供证据证明宾夕法尼亚州，密歇根州，佐治亚州或其他州的违规行为足以抵制拜登在选举学院中的领先地位。现在YouTube说已经受够了。
 ＆＃34;我们将开始删除今天（或之后）随时上载的任何内容，指称广泛的欺诈或错误改变了2020年美国总统大选的结果，从而误导了人们，＆＃34;由Google拥有的服务宣布。
 YouTube承认，由于选举官员已在努力完成点票工作，因此先前已允许就当前选举的点票结果或过程发表有争议的观点。但是现在，特朗普的大部分法律挑战都已被排除在法庭之外，YouTube表示，拜登大选的合法性不再值得争论。
 YouTube表示，它将从今天开始执行此政策，并将在接下来的几周内逐步实施。
 但是，将完全禁止讨论选举争议。 YouTube允许＆＃34;教育，纪录片，科学或艺术＆＃34;讨论内容的视频，否则将被禁止。要属于此例外，观看者必须＆＃34;清楚地知道创作者的目的不是宣传或支持违反我们政策的内容。＆＃34;换句话说，只有在明确表明视频不支持那些理论的情况下，该视频才能讨论选举欺诈的主张。
 自上个月的选举以来，不同的平台对选举欺诈的主张采取了不同的方法。例如，Twitter允许特朗普发布关于选举欺诈的指控，但在每条推文上都贴上标签，警告称这些指控有争议。 Facebook也采取了类似的方法。当有人在Facebook上发布与选举结果有争议的内容时，Facebook有时会添加一条信息性通知，将用户指向官方选举结果。
note_en:
For the last month, President Donald Trump and his allies have tried to cast doubt on President-elect Joe Biden's victory in the presidential election. However, they've failed to produce evidence of irregularities in Pennsylvania, Michigan, Georgia, or other states sufficient to overcome Biden's substantial lead in the electoral college. Now YouTube says it has had enough.
 "We will start removing any piece of content uploaded today (or anytime after) that misleads people by alleging that widespread fraud or errors changed the outcome of the 2020 US Presidential election," the Google-owned service  announced.
 YouTube acknowledged that it had previously allowed the airing of "controversial views on the outcome or process of counting votes of a current election as election officials have worked to finalize counts." But now that most of Trump's legal challenges have been  thrown out of court, YouTube says that the legitimacy of Biden's election is no longer up for debate.
 YouTube says it will "begin enforcing this policy today, and will ramp up in the weeks to come."
 Discussion of election controversies won't be completely forbidden, however. YouTube allows "educational, documentary, scientific, or artistic" videos to discuss content that would otherwise be banned. To fall under this exception, it must be "clear to the viewer that the creator’s aim is not to promote or support the content that violates our policies." In other words, a video can discuss claims of election fraud only if it's clear that the video isn't endorsing those theories.
 Different platforms have taken different approaches to claims of election fraud since last month's election. Twitter, for example, allows Trump to tweet out allegations of election fraud but attaches a label to each tweet warning that the allegations are disputed. Facebook has taken a similar approach. When someone posts content disputing the election result to Facebook, Facebook sometimes adds an informational notice pointing users to official election results.
title: YouTube禁止声称特朗普获胜的视频
title_en: YouTube bans videos claiming Trump won

3月，随着Covid-19大流行病席卷全球，阿萨夫·拉帕波特（Assaf Rappaport）努力说服主要公司，他的新网络安全公司将成为热门新事物。刚离开他的职位，成为微软在以色列的高管后，这笔赌注需要得到回报。 “我们正在与CISO会面，握手，” Rappaport说。时机不会更糟。
 现在，在编写第一行代码后仅九个月，Rappaport表示，他和他在Wiz的团队已从一些硅谷最杰出的投资者那里获得了1亿美元的融资，这是一家初创公司进行的较大规模的第一轮融资之一在2020年。
 基于湾区的Wiz借助软件工具脱颖而出，Rappaport声称该软件工具可以在15分钟内将其企业客户安装到企业客户中，从而使他们可以查看整个云网络中的IT威胁。 Wiz说，该公司已经获得了30多家企业客户，风险投资公司Index Ventures，Sequoia，Insight Partners和Cyber​​starts都加入了其A轮融资。福布斯》估计这笔资金可能使Wiz估值约10亿美元。红杉资本全球管理合伙人道格·利昂（Doug Leone）说，这样的猜测“不会太遥远”。
  Wiz的惊人增长来自对网络安全服务的旺盛需求，该领域在大流行期间蓬勃发展，因为企业在从本地网络迁移到云的过程中努力保护其网络安全。研究公司Gartner最近预测，信息安全和风险管理市场的最终用户支出将以8.2％的复合年增长率增长，到2024年将成为2077亿美元的市场。Proofpoint，Splunk和人群罢工都报告了今年对服务的需求不断增长。
 拉帕波特及其共同创始人阿米·卢特瓦克（Ami Luttwak），伊农·科斯蒂卡（Yinon Costica）和罗伊·雷兹尼克（Roy Reznik）都在30年代末期，在以色列国防军的精明情报部门8200部队首次会面，该部队相当于美国国家安全局。离开IDF之后，这四个人于2012年创立了Adallom，这是一家帮助客户管理多个云安全应用程序的创业公司。
 Adallom取得了快速的成功，微软在2015年以3.2亿美元的价格将其收购，任命其创始人领导Azure的云安全小组。在微软，Rappaport表示，他越来越意识到大公司面临的一个日益严重的问题：管理云安全威胁是一个分散的过程，安全团队变得不知所措。考虑到这一点，Rappaport宣布他去年12月离开微软开始成立另一家公司，他的Adallom团队很快也效仿。
 Wiz表示，自三月开始以来，它已经获得了多家美国最大的公司作为客户的收入；该公司将DocuSign命名为一位著名客户。除了Wiz声称可以在几分钟内完成客户注册外，Rappaport表示，该公司正在为企业IT安全团队提供一个单一的平台，使他们可以扫描整个云环境中的漏洞，风险和身份问题，而不是使用多种工具。 
Wiz在Shardul Shah身上找到了一位敏锐的投资者，Shardul Shah是Index Ventures的合伙人，曾领导该公司的投资，此前曾投资过Adallom。 当被问及Index在Wiz的第一轮融资中为何如此之大时，Shah指出，一般的初创公司通常需要35到40个月才能产生200万美元的年收入。 莎阿（Shah）说：“维兹花了不到六个。” “这是完全不同的坡度。”  该公司今天有30名员工。 Insight Partners的Leone，Shah和Jeff Horing以及Cyberstarts的Gili Raanan将加入Wiz的董事会。  在红杉，Leone说，鉴于Wiz的创始人团队和产品成熟，遵循典型的初创公司资金手册毫无意义。 “为什么要进行少量投资，您要找出什么？” 利昂说。 “投资他们，让他们摇滚-释放他们。”
note_en:
In March, as the Covid-19 pandemic was sweeping the globe, Assaf Rappaport was pushing hard to convince major companies that his new cybersecurity company was going to be the hot new thing. Having just left his post as a Microsoft executive in Israel, the bet needed to pay off. “We were meeting CISOs, shaking hands,” Rappaport says. The timing couldn’t have felt worse.
 Now, just nine months after writing their first lines of code, Rappaport says that he and his team at Wiz have landed $100 million in funding from some of Silicon Valley’s most prominent investors, in one of the larger first-year funding rounds for a startup in 2020.
 Bay Area-based Wiz is coming out of stealth with a software tool that Rappaport claims can onboard its enterprise clients in 15 minutes, giving them visibility into IT threats across an entire cloud network. The company has already secured more than 30 corporate customers, Wiz says, with venture capital firms Index Ventures, Sequoia, Insight Partners and Cyberstarts all joining its series A funding round.  Forbes estimates that the funding likely values Wiz around half a billion dollars; Sequoia global managing partner Doug Leone says such a guess “wouldn’t be far off.”
  Wiz’s dramatic rise comes amid white-hot demand for cybersecurity services, a sector that has boomed during the pandemic as corporations try to secure their networks as they move from on-premise networks to the cloud. Research firm Gartner  recently forecasted that end-user spending for the information security and risk management market will grow at a compound annual growth rate of 8.2%, becoming a $207.7 billion market by 2024. Publicly-traded cloud cybersecurity companies such as Proofpoint, Splunk and Crowdstrike have each reported growing demand for services this year.
 Rappaport and his co-founders — Ami Luttwak, Yinon Costica, and Roy Reznik — all in their late 30s, first met in Unit 8200, an elite intelligence division of the Israel Defense Forces, equivalent to the National Security Agency in the United States. After leaving the IDF, the four men started Adallom in 2012, a startup that helped customers manage multiple cloud security applications.
 Adallom was a fast success, and Microsoft acquired it for $320 million in 2015, appointing its founders to lead Azure’s Cloud Security Group. At Microsoft, Rappaport says he became increasingly aware of a growing problem for large companies: managing cloud security threats was a fragmented process, with security teams becoming overwhelmed by alerts. With that in mind, Rappaport announced he was leaving Microsoft last December to start another company, and his Adallom team soon followed suit.
 Since its March start, Wiz says it has secured several of the U.S.’ largest companies by revenue as clients; it named DocuSign as one notable customer. In addition to Wiz’s claim that it can onboard clients in minutes, Rappaport says the company is providing corporate IT security teams with a singular platform that allows them to scan an entire cloud environment for vulnerabilities, risks and identity issues, as opposed to using multiple tools.
  Wiz found a keen investor in Shardul Shah, the partner at Index Ventures who led the firm’s investment, and previously invested in Adallom. When asked why Index went so big on Wiz’s first funding round, Shah pointed out that the average startup typically takes between 35 and 40 months to generate $2 million in annual revenue. “It’s taken Wiz less than six,” Shah says. “It’s a completely different slope.”
 The company consists of 30 employees today. Leone, Shah, and Jeff Horing at Insight Partners and Gili Raanan of Cyberstarts will join Wiz’s board.
 At Sequoia, Leone says that given Wiz’s well-established founder group and product, there was little point in following the typical startup funding playbook. “Why are you going to make a small investment, what are you going to find out?” Leone says. “Invest in them and let them rock and roll — unleash them.”
title: Wiz帮助公司识别整个云网络中的网络安全威胁，以Index Ventures的名义筹集了1亿美元，估值为5亿美元
title_en: Wiz, which helps companies identify cybersecurity threats across a cloud network, raises $100M Series A led by Index Ventures at an estimated $500M valuation

note:
企业学习平台WorkRamp在由OMERS Ventures 1领导的B轮融资中筹集了1700万美元。
  WorkRamp旨在破坏传统的学习管理空间。该公司最初是一个入门产品，是一个平台，可以将传统上无聊的任务（例如合规性培训）转变为一口大小的学习活动。
 该公司的平台可用于公司内部和外部使用，因此企业可以将其用于员工的继续培训或教育他们的客户。
 随着COVID-19大流行以及如此多的在家工作的人，可以说，好的培训工具比以往任何时候都更加重要。
 首席执行官Ted Blosser在接受Crunchbase News采访时说：“这种流行病确实加速了所有公司的数字化转型，我们在说'嘿，要做到这一点，必须确保员工做好准备。”
 Blosser表示，该公司计划将投资重点放在其产品上，并正在努力改进WorkRamp的内部培训和客户培训。它还通过建立客户结果团队和专业的结果团队来投资于客户成功。客户成功团队中的这两个新团队旨在成为组织中的“迷你德勤”或“迷你埃森哲”。
 在扩展方面，WorkRamp专注于欧洲（其增长最快的市场之一）和印度。该公司计划在明年左右将员工人数增加一倍，从30多岁的员工人数增加到2021年底的大约80名。 
今年，WorkRamp的产品使用率和收入也在加速增长。 Blosser表示，在2020年的前9个月中，该公司的产品使用率整体增长了两倍，今年迄今为止的同比收入增长了一倍以上。 WorkRamp的客户中包括Zoom，Box和PayPal等公司。
 Blosser表示：“我们刚刚看到在这种环境下接受培训的人数正在加速增长，与此同时，我们看到人们将他们的多个学习平台整合到一个平台上。”
 Blosser说，该公司不想筹款，但是OMERS Ventures的Eugene Lee希望投资一家学习提供商，并创立了WorkRamp。
 “ OMERS是与之合作的好公司，” Blosser说。 “我们与合伙人Eugene Lee一起工作，而他实际上是在寻找人才，因为他们的投资策略非常受论文驱动……他正在寻找下一个优秀的学习提供商LMS提供商。”
 Blosser回忆起打给Lee的第一通电话之一，并告诉他的联合创始人，他可能找到了一个比他对学习管理领域了解更多的投资者。
 Lee在一份声明中说：“这种大流行迫使几乎所有行业的客户和员工都采用了数字优先的方法。” “ WorkRamp的平台是增强当今和未来这两个重要受众的基础。”
 新一轮的融资使WorkRamp的总资金超过2700万美元。该公司最近一次融资是在2019年6月进行了800万美元的A轮融资。 
OMERS Ventures是Crunchbase的投资者。 在我们的编辑过程中没有发言权。 有关更多信息，请前往此处。 ↩
note_en:
Enterprise learning platform   WorkRamp  has raised $17 million in a Series B round led by   OMERS Ventures  1.
  WorkRamp is aiming to disrupt the traditional learning management space. The company, which started as an onboarding product, is a platform that turns traditionally boring tasks, like compliance training, into bite-sized learning activities.
 The company’s platform is meant for both a company’s internal and external uses, so a business can use it for continuing training for employees or to educate their customers.
 With the COVID-19 pandemic and so many people working from home, good tools for training are arguably more important than ever.
 “The pandemic has really accelerated digital transformation for all companies and we’re saying, ‘Hey, in order to do that, you have to make sure your people are ready,’” CEO  Ted Blosser said in an interview with Crunchbase News.
 The company plans to focus the investment on its product and is working on improving WorkRamp for both internal training and for customer education, Blosser said. It’s also investing in customer success by building out a client outcomes team and a professional outcomes team. Those two new teams within the customer success team are intended to be like a “mini Deloitte” or “mini Accenture” within the organization.
 In terms of expansion, WorkRamp is focusing on Europe, one of its fastest-growing markets, and India. The company plans on doubling its headcount over the next year or so, going from an employee base in the high-30s to about 80 by the end of 2021.
 WorkRamp has seen its product usage and revenue accelerate this year, as well. In the first nine months of 2020, the company tripled its product usage across the board, Blosser said, with year-over-year revenue more than doubled so far this year. WorkRamp counts companies like  Zoom,  Box and  PayPal among its customers.
 “We’ve just seen the acceleration of how much people are training in this environment and with that we’re seeing people consolidate their multiple learning platforms onto one platform,” Blosser said.
 The company wasn’t looking to fundraise, Blosser said, but   Eugene Lee  of OMERS Ventures was looking to invest in a learning provider and found WorkRamp.
 “OMERS was a great firm to partner (with),” Blosser said. “We worked with a partner, Eugene Lee and he was literally on the hunt because they’re very thesis-driven in terms of their investment strategy … he was on the hunt for the next great learning provider, LMS provider.”
 Blosser recalled getting off one of the first calls with Lee and telling his co-founder that he may have found an investor who knew more about the learning management space than he did.
 “The pandemic has forced adoption of a digital-first approach towards customers and employees across virtually all industries,” Lee said in a statement. “WorkRamp’s platform is foundational to empowering both of these important audiences today and in the future.”
 The new round brings WorkRamp’s total funding to more than $27 million. The company last raised money with an $8 million   Series A  in June 2019.
  OMERS Ventures is an investor in Crunchbase. It has no say in our editorial process. For more,  head here. ↩
title: 提供数字工作场所培训工具的WorkRamp筹集了由OMERS Ventures牵头的1,700万美元B轮融资，并将专注于欧洲和印度的增长
title_en: WorkRamp, which offers digital workplace training tools, raises $17M Series B led by OMERS Ventures and will focus on European and Indian growth

note:
去年春天，随着世界陷入停滞，全球经济陷入螺旋形发展，一家公司取得了成功。一个名为Robinhood的基于电话的交易应用程序开始与诸如​​Charles Schwab，TD Ameritrade和E-Trade等更著名的在线经纪对手竞争。大流行开始的那一刻，似乎世界开始在Robinhood上交易股票。
 数字惊人。 Robinhood的平均每日交易量在今年第一季度比2019年最后一个季度增长了两倍，并且由于数百万人失业，净存款增加了十倍。据《纽约时报》报道，该公司第一季度的交易量是E-Trade的9倍，是Schwab的40倍。它增加了300万个新客户帐户，到6月，其每日平均收入交易（DARTS）达到了430万笔，超过任何其他在线公司，也超过了嘉信理财和E-Trade的总和。
 在包括Kleiner Perkins，NEA，红杉，Thrive Capital，Ribbit Capital和Google风险投资部门GV在内的一系列风险投资公司的支持下，该公司获得了四笔主要现金注入。投资者在4月向该公司注资2亿美元，7月增加了3.2亿美元，8月又增加了2亿美元，最后在11月又增加了4.6亿美元。在这段短暂的时间内，该公司的市值从82亿美元跃升至117亿美元，当时有消息说该公司“要求银行出任角色”以进行明年的IPO。
 分析师只看到了征服。彭博资讯（Bloomberg Intelligence）市场结构研究主管拉里·塔布（Larry Tabb）说：“与罗宾汉进行竞争将很困难。”
 罗宾汉似乎是21世纪资本主义的新浪子，是华尔街和硅谷的绝妙混合体。该公司将高盛（Goldman），萨克斯（Sachs）或摩根大通（JP Morgan Chase）的纯粹贪婪与Instagram或TikTok的欢快，青年友好的用户互动策略结合在一起。该公司成立于2013年，是由两名永驻斯坦福大学的毕业生组成，名叫Baiju Bhatt和Vlad Tenev，他们穿着卡其布和Monkees发型，相距不远。
  这些看上去无害的蛋头听起来实在令人兴奋，可以将其产品推向世界，说明他们的使命是“使所有人的金融民主化”。
 该应用程序专为实现这种“民主化”而设计。它是免费的，不收取任何交易佣金。它还进行了类似乔·骆驼（Joe Camel）的诱人营销活动，以免费样品共享赠品，“刮刮”奖励和视频彩纸庆祝交易的形式展示了许多风声。 《华尔街日报》的杰森·斯威格（Jason Sweig）就是这样说的：“甚至可以招募最怀疑的投资者，”他描述了如何在不到一周的时间里更多地了解“罗宾汉”的经历导致了类似的成瘾。 
买不起全部亚马逊股票的小型客户可以购买零碎股票，也可以像专业人士一样立即进行复杂的期权下注！彭博社所说的“在像游戏一样有趣的手机应用上进行股票交易”将成群的菜鸟带入了市场：罗宾汉今年的客户中有一半是首次交易者，其管理的资产中有80％属于千禧一代。
 该公司是大流行时代成功的标志，是金融界对Netflix和Amazon Prime的回应。没有体育运动可以赌注，也没有酒吧爬网，新一代的“投资者”使Robinhood成为了最终的Covid-19新瘾：狂欢交易的目的地。将更多的人引入股票市场可能会出什么问题？
 事实证明，很多。 Themis Trading的乔·萨卢齐（Joe Saluzzi）说：“每次有人说他们想'民主化获取'，我都会非常害怕。”
  6月12日，内布拉斯加大学（University of Nebraska）的20岁学生亚历山大·卡恩斯（Alexander Kearns）看着他的应用，发现他认为有730,000美元的负余额。误解了读数，仅显示了他进行的一系列期权交易中的一部分，凯恩斯错误地认为自己在财务上已经破产。他走上火车，自杀身亡，留下一条纸条问：“一个20岁的老人，没有收入，怎么能获得将近一百万美元的杠杆作用？”
  Kearns似乎是个本来很幸福的年轻人。亲戚比尔·布鲁斯特（Bill Brewster）说：“在感恩节，我可以把我的孩子留给亚历克斯（Alex），当他与父亲交谈时，他会整天招待他们。” “他是个好孩子。”
 布鲁斯特说，他最后一次与亚历克斯交谈是在三月。 “可悲的是，出于正确的理由，他对金融感到非常兴奋。不只是钱。他对这一切的运作方式非常感兴趣。”
 Kearns的去世引发了可预见的愤怒，批评者集中于缺乏对试图直接进入复杂交易的新用户的控制和指导，更糟糕的是，几乎完全没有人类客户服务选择。消费者金融保护局数据库中有许多客户抱怨称无法及时与公司联系。如果您有一个紧急问题，例如认为您欠一百万美元的四分之三，那就太糟糕了：Robinhood的主要反馈选项是电子邮件，回复时间最多为一周。 
该公司表示：“虽然历史上我们发现我们可以通过电子邮件为客户提供最好的服务，但它也”正在为关键问题探索新的渠道。”但是，与此同时，请通过电子邮件发送这些问题。
  不久，观察者开始提出更深层次的问题。为什么公司会如此吸引顾客进入期权交易？为什么要强调年轻的，没有经验的用户？不收取费用的公司如何赚钱？
 最后一个问题是关键。在媒体报道中使用“有争议但合法的做法”这样的术语来委婉地描述时，Robinhood将大部分资金用于“订单流支付”。它把数据卖给Citadel和Virtu这样的高频交易者，做市商显然是为为Robinhood投资者进行交易而付出了代价。这些公司采用了著名的迈克尔·刘易斯（Michael Lewis）的书《闪电男孩》（Flash Boys）的技术，利用复杂的算法领先于其他交易者，以寻找微小的价格差异并衡量市场情绪和供求状况。
 Robinhood投资者输入交易只是开始一系列交易，可能会导致一连串的参与者得到补偿。 Robinhood不会也不能直接将订单发布到纳斯达克等交易所。收取费用后，它将所有订单发送给Citadel或Virtu等做市商公司。这些公司反过来又在Robinhood交易员的订单上拥有免费期权。他们可以自己执行交易，也可以将其卸给交易所，后者再下达订单并以回扣的形式补偿做市商公司，同时通过对“数据提要”收取费用来赚钱，其中包括这样的零售订单。
 所有这些机制都不是Robinhood客户理解所必需的，但是值得一提的问题是，Robinhood客户与其交易之间的所有这些参与者是否比传统经纪人撤回了更多或更少的价值。费用。负责处理Robinhood大部分业务的HFT公司始终坚称，他们的所作所为对社会有利，因为他们的交易“增加了流动性”并提高了市场效率。批评者则相反，高速算法交易只是利用对市场情报的先睹为快，将交易转变为低风险的类似套利活动。
 无论如何，“订单流支付”并不是Robinhood独有的。电子贸易，嘉信理财和其他公司也这样做。由于在所有情况下都不清楚购买者如何使用他们的信息，因此有时很难具体确定这些交易的道德规范。但是，在Robinhood的情况下，需要注意三个事实：
 无论HFT公司为订单流程支付多少费用，对他们而言在财务上都是值得的。如果他们为您的交易支付了50美分，那么他们的收益就超过了50美分。 
Robinhood的流量越来越大。 2020年第一季度，Citadel，Virtu和Wolverine等公司向他们支付了9,100万美元。在第二季度，这一数字跃升至1.8亿美元。
 这些公司为Robinhood的订单流支付的费用比为其他公司的订单流支付的费用要高得多：根据彭博社的分析，它们平均要多支付17％。
 可能会添加一些其他细节，例如Tenev和Bhatt，这是在创建Robinhood之前，该公司为高频交易者设计了算法软件。正如Saluzzi所说，“这些人知道管道的工作原理。”
 此外，Robinhood的薪酬模型不同于E-Trade和其他公司。投资银行Piper Sandler在今年夏天所做的分析表明，“ Robinhood的固定价差是固定的（相对于其他eBrokers的固定价为每股）。” Robinhood不会按数量收取简单的付款，而是会在每个交易中获得买价和卖价之间的价差百分比。
 这很有趣，因为尽管高频交易的支持者坚持其做法是缩小价差，但一些批评家认为，高频交易最终会扩大价差。例如，在萨卢齐（Saluzzi）的《市场破损》一书中，他估计“虽然最活跃交易的名称中约5％的点差较小”，但“其他95％的市场”中的点差却更大。
 一个玩世不恭的人可能会考虑所有这一切，并假设接受过构建高频交易软件培训的工程师可以对零售股票交易应用进行逆向工程，该应用可以满足两个目的。一方面，它将以经验不足的“傻钱”投资者的形式为经验丰富的交易者提供最有价值的订单流，同时设计一种最能利用HFT交易者操作方式的补偿模型。
 罗宾汉显然在所有这些问题上都有不同的看法。它辩称，并且做市商也同意，这是它为自己的交易支付比其他公司更多的主要原因，这与客户交易规模相对较小有关。该公司对TK表示：“对于做市商而言，执行较小的订单比将10,000股定单纳入补偿模型的风险要低。”换句话说，像Citadel或Wolverine这样的公司宁愿在俄克拉荷马州处理一个小孩，从他的地下室交易100股股票，而不是经验丰富的经纪人可能会甩掉一笔小交易，但要有足够的资金来影响市场下一个以不可预测的方式他们说，Robinhood投资者才是更安全的生意。 
无论您对无佣金交易的功效有何看法，在大流行期间向千禧一代推销基于电话的交易应用程序的潜在收入是显而易见的。正如《纽约时报》和其他报道所报道的那样，罗宾汉的投资者之一是演员阿什顿·库彻（Ashton Kutcher），他于6月参加了该公司的Zoom会议。据报道，在那次会议上，他通过将公司与赌博网站进行比较，对公司的潜力进行了猜测。库彻（Kutcher）发表声明说，他“不是在暗示Robinhood是一个赌博平台”，而是指该公司的“当前增长指标”。
 罗宾汉（Robinhood）也说了同样的话，它建立了自己的平台，以“使新一代的投资更容易获得，并使首次投资的人成为长期投资者。”该公司坚称，“区分可访问的，现代的设计和游戏化很重要，”该公司坚持其研究表明，随着时间的流逝，“我们的大多数客户都采用并购策略。”
 Brewster不会购买。他说：“一切都旨在使投资看起来像DraftKings。”此外，该公司在客户的股票上涨5％或下跌10％或下跌10％时提供推送通知的做法可能会触发产生多巴胺的无尽循环，将FOMO / clickbait心理与博彩冲动相结合。客户认为，“如果天气不好，我得走了。如果涨了，我得买更多。”布鲁斯特说。
 明显的问题是，这些年轻客户中有很多都不知道他们在做什么。 Saluzzi叹息道：“零售投资者不了解股票，更不用说期权了。”他将这项服务与将业余扑克玩家带到维加斯（Vegas），而不是与老太太和游客坐在一张桌子上，而是与镇上最好的玩家坐在一张桌子上进行比较。他说：“这将它们与鲨鱼混在一起。”
 但是，甚至一些公司的批评者也存在冲突，他们不想反对任何人立即跳入深渊的自由。有些人持更加积极的看法。 Bailout Nation的作者和Ritholtz Wealth Management的首席执行官Barry Ritholtz认为Robinhood只是原始在线交易平台的现代化身，该平台通过同样承诺“使经纪人民主化”而成为流行文化的流行。”他回想起了90年代著名的Ameritrade广告“让蜡烛点亮”：
  里特霍兹（Ritholtz）认为，只要年轻的投资者有充分的准备面对面，罗宾汉就是伟大的。他说，至关重要的是，他们只能冒险“赚钱”，并且要让自己的余额达到零才能安心参加活动。他说，这甚至可能有价值。
 Ritholtz说：“如果他们把驴交给他们，那是一门教训，比起晚生活，要早得多。”他补充说，一些人甚至可能从罗宾汉的行列中崛起。他说：“少数人会发现，‘嘿，我可以打三分跳投’。” 
人们经常猜测，罗宾汉取得成功的部分原因在于，它吸引了一个千禧一代的人口，由于经济萎缩，它在政治上关注的是它是否会或将在未来拥有任何利益的问题。 Ritholtz说：“与我们这一代人相比，他们拥有更多的主人翁意识。” “是，‘我想成为社会经济方面的合作伙伴。’”
 他认为，这很棒，尽管他和其他老牌投资者确实担心，当市场下跌时，罗宾汉的客户会发生什么。他说：“众所周知，‘永远不要把牛市与大脑混淆。”标普500指数自3月23日的低点以来上涨了近64％，就在Robinhood的数字开始迅速攀升之时。
 其他人则更为悲观。一位投资顾问这样说：“这不会很好地结束。” “这些新投资者中有很多人认为自己做得很好，但是他们就像Wile E. Coyote一样-可能已经走下坡路，但是由于美联储的帮助，他们还没有表现出来。”
 Robinhood正在接受SEC的调查，表面上是因为未能完全披露其销售订单流程的做法。 《华尔街日报》上月表示，该公司“可能必须支付超过1000万美元的罚款”，考虑到该公司已经赚到的钱，这听起来不算多，而且El Dorado仍然有望赢得一笔罚款。潜在的IPO。去年，罗宾汉因未遵守“最佳执行”规则而被金融业监管局（FINRA）罚款，该规则要求成员所采取合理措施为客户找到最佳价格。
 如果以及当IPO资金到来时，Robinhood将成为Facebook的财务版本：这是一个免费平台，可让大量的客户参与到超级刺激的用户体验中，同时通过赚钱将有关这些客户行为的情报出售给另一端是专业的财富提取者。
 它是完美的捕鼠器，除了它的名字外，还具有其他优点。 “那是另一回事，”布鲁斯特说。 “他们叫罗宾汉。”美国版本并没有从富人那里窃取钱财，而是向穷人提供了钱，而是吸收了年轻人，并将其出售给计算机驱动的对冲基金。这个罗宾汉是永远赢家的房子。如果说在科维德时代有一个更出色的资本主义隐喻，那很难想象。
note_en:
As the world went into lockdown and the global economy into a spiral last spring, one company struck gold. A phone-based trading app called Robinhood began wiping the floor with more celebrated online brokerage rivals like Charles Schwab, TD Ameritrade, and E-Trade. The moment the pandemic began, it seemed, the world started trading stocks on Robinhood.
 The numbers were staggering. Robinhood’s average daily trading  volume tripled in the first quarter of this year, compared with the last quarter of 2019, and saw a tenfold increase in net deposits as millions were losing their jobs. The  New York Times reported that the firm in the first quarter traded nine times as many shares as E-Trade, and an incredible 40 times as many as Schwab. It added 3 million new customer accounts, and by June was doing  4.3 million daily average revenue trades, or DARTS, more than any other online firm and more than Schwab and E-Trade combined.
 Backed by a string of venture capital firms, including Kleiner Perkins, NEA, Sequoia, Thrive Capital, Ribbit Capital, and Google’s VC arm, GV, the firm received four major cash injections. Investors  poured $200 million into the firm in April, $320 million more in July, another $200 million in August, and finally in November, another $460 million. Through this brief time, the company’s valuation jumped from $8.2 billion to $11.7 billion, by which time word leaked out that the firm had “asked banks to pitch for roles” for a possible IPO next year.
 Analysts saw nothing but conquest ahead. “Competing versus Robinhood will be difficult,”  said Larry Tabb, head of market structure research for Bloomberg Intelligence.
 Robinhood seemed a new prodigal son of 21st-century capitalism, an awesome hybrid of Wall Street and Silicon Valley. The firm combined the pure greed of a Goldman, Sachs or JP Morgan Chase with the cheery, youth-friendly user-engagement strategies of Instagram or TikTok. The firm was founded in 2013 by two perma-smiling Stanford grads named  Baiju Bhatt and Vlad Tenev, who wore khakis and Monkees haircuts and never seemed more than a moment away from  bro-hugging one another.
  These harmless-looking eggheads sounded genuinely excited to bring their product to the world, explaining they had a mission to “ democratize finance for all.”
 The app is perfectly designed for such “democratization.” It’s free, charging no commissions for trades. It also has an alluring, Joe Camel-like marketing campaign, featuring a host of bells and whistles in the form of free sample share giveaways, “scratch-off” rewards, and video confetti to celebrate transactions. “Even the most skeptical investor can be drawn in,” is how Jason Sweig of the  Wall Street Journal just   put it, describing how an assignment to learn more about the Robinhood experience led to something like addiction in less than a week.
 Small-time customers who can’t afford a whole share of, say, Amazon stock can buy fractional stocks, and can also engage right away in complex options bets, just like the pros! What  Bloomberg  called “stock trading on a fun gamelike phone app” brings hordes of rookies into the markets: half of Robinhood’s customers this year were first-time traders, and 80% of its assets under management belong to millennials.
 The firm is an icon of success in the pandemic age, finance’s answer to Netflix and Amazon Prime. Without sports to bet on, or bars to crawl, a whole new generation of “investors” are making Robinhood the destination for the ultimate new Covid-19 addiction: binge-trading. What could possibly go wrong with bringing more people into the stock market?
 A lot, as it turns out. “Every time someone says they want to ‘democratize access,’” says Joe Saluzzi of Themis Trading, “I get very scared.”
  On June 12th, a 20-year-old University of Nebraska student named Alexander Kearns looked at his app and saw what he thought was a negative balance of $730,000. Misunderstanding the readout, which only showed part of a series of options trades he’d made, Kearns mistakenly believed he’d been ruined financially. He  killed himself by stepping in front of a train, leaving behind a note asking, “How was a 20-year-old with no income able to get assigned almost a million dollar’s worth of leverage?”
  Kearns appears to have been an otherwise happy young man. “At Thanksgiving, I could leave my kids with Alex, and he’d entertain them all day while I talked to his Dad,” says Bill Brewster, a relative. “He was a great kid.”
 Brewster says the last time he talked to Alex was in March. “The sad thing is, he was really excited about finance, and for the right reasons. It wasn’t just about money. He was really interested in how it all works.”
 The death of Kearns prompted predictable  outrage, with critics focusing on a lack of controls and guidance for new users seeking to rush straight into complex trades, and, worse, a near-total absence of human customer service options. The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau database has many complaints from customers  claiming an inability to contact the firm in timely fashion. If you have an urgent question, like for instance thinking you owe three-quarters of a million dollars, too bad: Robinhood’s chief feedback option is email, with response times of up to a week.
 The company says that “while historically we’ve found we can best serve customers over email,” it’s also “exploring new channels for critical issues.” In the meantime, however, email those questions.
  Soon, observers began asking deeper questions. Why is the company so game for customers to get into options trades? Why the emphasis on young, inexperienced users? How does a company that doesn’t charge fees make money?
 The last question is key. In what the press accounts describe euphemistically using terms like a “ controversial but legal practice,” Robinhood makes the bulk of its money on “payment for order flow.” It sells its data to high-frequency traders like Citadel and Virtu, market makers who ostensibly are paying for the honor of executing trades for Robinhood investors. These firms, using the technology that’s the subject of the celebrated Michael Lewis book   Flash Boys, hunt out tiny price differences and gauge market sentiment and supply and demand before other traders, using sophisticated algorithms to jump ahead of the pack.
 A Robinhood investor typing in a trade is just beginning a series of transactions that might result in a string of actors being compensated. Robinhood does not and can not post orders directly to exchanges like the NASDAQ; for a fee, it sends all of its orders to market maker firms like Citadel or Virtu. Those firms in turn have what amounts to a free option on the Robinhood trader’s order. They can execute the trade themselves, or they can offload it to an exchange, which in turn posts the order and compensates the market maker firm in the form of rebates, while earning money itself by charging fees for “data feed” that include information about such retail orders.
 The mechanics of all of this are not absolutely necessary for Robinhood customers to understand, but it is worth asking the question of whether all of these actors in between the Robinhood client and his or her trades withdraw more or less value than, say, traditional broker fees. The HFT firms that handle the bulk of Robinhood’s business have always maintained that what they do is socially beneficial, because their trades “ add liquidity” and make markets more efficient. Critics  say the opposite, that high-speed algorithmic trading is just using advance peeks at market intelligence to turn trading into a low-risk arbitrage-like activity.
 At any rate, “payment for order flow” isn’t unique to Robinhood. E-Trade, Schwab, and others also do it. Because it’s not clear in all cases how purchasers use their information, it’s hard sometimes to make a concrete determination about the ethics of these transactions. In the case of Robinhood, however, three facts are worth noting:
 Whatever HFT firms are paying for order flow, it’s worth it to them financially. If they’re paying 50 cents for your trade, they’re making more than 50 cents.
 Robinhood is getting gargantuan sums for its flow. In the first quarter of 2020, firms like Citadel, Virtu, and Wolverine paid them $91 million. In the second quarter, that number  jumped to $180 million.
 These firms pay more significantly more for Robinhood’s order flow than they do for the order flow of other firms: an  average of 17% more, according to a  Bloomberg analysis.
 One might add a few other details, like that Tenev and Bhatt, prior to founding Robinhood,  designed algorithmic software for high-frequency traders. As Saluzzi puts it, “these guys know how the plumbing works.”
 Also, Robinhood’s compensation model differs from E-Trade and other firms. As an  analysis by the investment bank Piper Sandler put it this summer, “Robinhood receives a fixed rate per spread (vs. a fixed rate per share by the other eBrokers).” Rather than receiving simple payment by volume, Robinhood receives a percentage of the spread between the bid and the ask in each trade.
 This is interesting because while HFT proponents insist their practices narrow spreads, some critics maintain that high-frequency trading ends up widening spreads. In Saluzzi’s book “ Broken Markets,” for instance, he estimates that while spreads are narrower in “perhaps 5% of the most actively traded names,” they’re wider in “the other 95% of the market.”
 A cynical person might take all this in and hypothesize that engineers trained in building high-frequency trading software could reverse-engineer a retail stock-trading app that would serve two purposes. On the one hand, it would provide sophisticated traders with the most valuable kind of order flow in the form of inexperienced “ dumb money” investors, while designing a compensation model best equipped to take advantage of how HFT traders operate.
 Robinhood obviously has a different take on all of these issues. It argues, and market maker firms agree, that the primary reason it’s paid more for its flow than other firms has to do with the relatively small size of its customers’ trades. “For a market maker,” the firm told  TK, “executing a smaller order is lower risk compared to a 10,000 share order, which is baked into the compensation model.” In other words, a firm like Citadel or Wolverine would rather deal with a kid in Oklahoma trading a hundred shares out of his basement, as opposed to an experienced broker that might dangle one small trade, but be backed by enough money to impact the market in unpredictable ways with the next one. The Robinhood investor, they say, is just safer business.
 Whatever you believe about the efficacy of commission-free trading, the revenue potential of a phone-based trading app marketed to millennials in the middle of a pandemic is obvious. As the  New York Times and others reported, one of Robinhood’s investors is the actor Ashton Kutcher, who  attended a Zoom meeting for the firm in June. In that meeting, he reportedly gushed about the company’s potential by comparing it to gambling websites. Kutcher put out a statement that he was “not insinuating that Robinhood is a gambling platform,” but rather referring to the company’s “current growth metrics.”
 Robinhood says the same thing, that it built its platform to “make investing more accessible to a new generation and to make first-time investors into long-term investors.” Adding, “it’s important to distinguish between accessible, modern design and gamification,” the company insists its research shows that over time, “most of our customers use a buy-and-hold strategy.”
 Brewster isn’t buying it. “Everything is designed to make investing look like DraftKings,” he says. Moreover, the company’s practice of offering push notifications when customers’ stocks  move up or down 5% or 10% could trigger an endless cycle of dopamine-generating responses, combining FOMO/clickbait psychology with the betting urge. Customers think, “if it’s down, I gotta get out. If it’s up, I gotta buy more,” says Brewster.
 The obvious problem is that a lot of these younger customers have no clue what they’re doing. “Retail investors don’t understand stocks, let alone options,” sighs Saluzzi. He compares the service to bringing amateur poker players to Vegas and seating them not at a table with old ladies and tourists, but with the best players in town. “It’s throwing them right in with the sharks,” he says.
 Even some of the firm’s detractors are conflicted, however, not wanting to argue against anyone’s freedom to jump in the deep end right away. Some take an even more positive view. Barry Ritholtz, author of  Bailout Nation and CEO of Ritholtz Wealth Management, thinks Robinhood is just the modern incarnation of the original online trading platforms that became pop culture hits by similarly promising to “ democratize” brokerage.” He recalls the famed “Let’s light this candle” ads for Ameritrade in the nineties:
  Ritholtz thinks Robinhood is great, so long as young investors are fully prepared to get decked in the face. It’s crucial, he says, that they only risk “fun money,” and enter into the activity being comfortable with their balance going to zero. There may even be value in that, he says.
 “If they get their asses handed to them, that’s a lesson that’s much better to learn early in life than later,” Ritholtz says. He adds that some talent might even rise from the Robinhood ranks. “A handful of people will discover, ‘Hey, I can hit a three-point jumper,’” is how he puts it.
 It’s often speculated that part of Robinhood’s success is that it appeals to a millennial demographic that, because of a shrinking economy, is politically concerned with the question of whether or not it can or will have any stake in the future. “They have more of an ownership mentality than my generation did,” Ritholtz says. “It’s, ‘I want to be a partner in the upside of the economics of society.’”
 Which is great, he thinks, although he and other old-school investors do worry what will happen to Robinhood’s customers when the markets go down. “It’s been famously said, ‘Never confuse a bull market with brains,’” he says, noting the S&P 500 is  up nearly 64% since lows on March 23, right around the time the Robinhood’s numbers began zooming skyward.
 Others are more pessimistic. “This isn’t going to end well,” is how one investment advisor puts it. “There are tons of these new investors who think they’re doing great, but they’re like Wile E. Coyote — probably off the cliff already, but thanks to the Fed, they just haven’t gone splat yet.”
 Robinhood is  under investigation by the SEC, ostensibly for failure to fully disclose its practice of selling order flow. The  Wall Street Journal said last month the firm “could have to pay a fine exceeding $10 million,” which doesn’t sound like much given the amount of money the firm has already made, and the El Dorado it still stands to win in a potential IPO. Robinhood was also  fined by the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (FINRA) last year for failure to abide by “best execution” rules, which require member firms to take reasonable measures to find the best prices for customers.
 If and when IPO money comes, Robinhood will be on its way to becoming a finance version of Facebook: a free platform that keeps a sea of customers engaged with a hyper-stimulating user experience, while making money selling intelligence about those customers’ behaviors to expert wealth extractors on other end.
 It’s the perfect mousetrap, among other things because of its name. “That’s the other thing,” says Brewster. “They call it  Robin Hood.” Instead of stealing from the rich and giving to the poor, the American version takes in the young and sells them to computer-powered hedge funds; this Robin Hood is the house that always wins. If there’s a more brilliant metaphor for capitalism in the Covid age, it’s hard to imagine.
title: 大流行反派：罗宾汉[马特·泰比]

note:
人力资源部的那个女人打开了门-那些带有推杆的重型重型防火门。她带路穿过一个没有照明的，没有窗户的房间，房间里挂着塑料，闻着油漆。克里斯蒂娜·巴兰（Cristina Balan）变得紧张起来。她心想：“这看起来不像是埃隆的办公室。”
 她说，她被引导穿过另一扇门进入一个房间，在那里她看到桌子后面有两个穿着安全制服的大个子男人。他们被命令在外面等。 Balan的老板走进来。被告知Balan坐在滚动的椅子上。他们由公司律师参加。
  几周前，在公司范围内的一封电子邮件中，特斯拉首席执行官埃隆·马斯克（Elon Musk）放弃了公司层次结构的概念。他告诉他们，找到“最快解决问题的方法，使整个公司受益”，不要担心您的老板。如有必要，直接转到顶部。
  Balan接过他。她说，她是电动汽车制造商位于加利福尼亚州弗里蒙特的装配厂的一名汽车工程师，她受到特斯拉的拯救地球任务的激励，并希望它能够成功。
 因此，她不愿看到自己认为是基于友谊而不是质量和价格的合同。她还一直对新的Model S中安装的地垫产生担忧，该地垫往往会在踏板下弯曲，这有潜在的安全隐患。垫子损坏导致其他汽车制造商的车辆坠毁。
  几天后，人事经理出现在她的办公桌前，说道：“所以你想和伊隆谈谈，对吗？好吧，走吧。”但是当Balan看到带有塑料窗帘的黑暗房间时，“我知道我应该回到我的办公桌上了，”她说。 “我觉得这很奇怪。这将不是与Elon的会议。”
    取而代之的是，Balan说，她被迫辞职，这一事件引发了长达六年的法律历程，直到今天她所处的位置：即将在美国第9巡回法院面对特斯拉-在没有律师的情况下独自面对特斯拉。上诉。 
诽谤是Balan起诉特斯拉的主要诉求-该公司指控她在一个流行的在线新闻网站上犯有犯罪行为，但没有提供证据支持这一指控。 Balan说，她的职业声誉受到严重损害，以至于找不到一家愿意雇用她的公司。她说：“他们告诉我，‘我们想雇用您，但我们无力进入马斯克的黑名单。’
 已经对特斯拉和马斯克提起了1,000多次诉讼，其中许多指控诽谤，其中包括马斯克称救助潜水员在Twitter上批评他为“脚踏车”和在给记者的电子邮件中批评为“儿童强奸犯”的案件。没有提供证据。 （洛杉矶陪审团裁定该潜水员没有受到诽谤。）
 但是Balan的上诉案不但遭到诽谤。这就引起了人们对以下问题的质疑：被称为强制性仲裁的法律实践的局限性，关于仲裁协议能否在雇员离开公司后的数年甚至数十年内遵循的原则？这就是特斯拉律师在试图维持案件时的观点。法庭之外。
  要求美国公司数以百万计的员工签署仲裁合同，而这些合同通常对他们所签署的内容一无所知。洛约拉大学新奥尔良法学院的仲裁专家Imre Szalai说，鉴于特斯拉的高知名度，“这将是仲裁和诽谤问题的领先案例。”
   克里斯蒂娜·巴兰（Cristina Balan）在罗马尼亚的特兰西瓦尼亚地区的指甲下长满了油脂。其他女孩玩洋娃娃。她将发动机和电动机拆开，然后又重新组装在一起。她说：“我对机械的一切都着迷了。” “我的玩具是钳子和螺丝刀。”
 那引起邻居们的咯咯笑声。她的父亲爱恩·卡斯托克（Ion Carstoc）当时是罗马尼亚国家汽车研究中心的一名技师，现已退休。 “我永远不会忘记，[邻居]叫我名字，告诉我要成为负责任的父母。她说，‘克里斯蒂娜（Cristina）的衣服上沾了油，头发上沾了油。您需要教她如何成为女人！’”
 巴兰耸了耸肩。在布拉索夫的特兰西瓦尼亚大学获得工程学学位后，她被波音公司聘请，环游世界，与787飞机的供应商合作。 2010年，特斯拉（Tesla）聘请她来帮助设计现今具有标志性的Model S全电动豪华轿车的电池组。 
根据《泰晤士报》（The Times）审查的证据，Balan得到了工人和管理人员的一致好评。她的表演评论令人眼前一亮。她以勤奋工作着称，她投入大量时间并致力于创造高质量的产品。
   她的名字首字母刻在她设计的翻盖式电池盒上，仍然可以在旧型号的Model S上找到。法庭上进行的性能审查称赞她说：“经常在不同部门的工程师咨询她。他们面临着复杂的设计和CAD挑战。” （CAD代表计算机辅助设计。）
 特斯拉高级执行官丹尼尔·何（Daniel Ho）在2014年发了一封电子邮件，他现在正在执行Model 3计划，他特别感谢Balan。他说：“没有像您这样的创意工程师，这个地方将只是另一个汽车公司。” “不要让发生！”何先生未回应置评请求。
     Balan加入室内装饰团队后开始出现问题。她开发了一种方法，可以使用基于触摸的挡风玻璃调光技术来代替上下翻转式遮阳板。特斯拉从未采用过它，但电子邮件显示该想法已由马斯克（Musk）审查，他敦促继续发展。
 直到Balan遇到了她认为与供应商合同和卷边地板垫有关的粗略交易，一切似乎都进展顺利。她说，当时的室内工程主管承认安全问题，但指出召回地板垫本来会很昂贵。
 她说，在将自己的担忧通过电子邮件发送给马斯克后，特斯拉的一位律师将她放在一边，并告诉她，如果她继续推动事务，她应该记住，她在特斯拉的一些朋友正在申请绿卡。 （《泰晤士报》联系该律师，否认了这一说法。）
 特斯拉当时在为偿债能力而挣扎。它尚未成为股票市场的宠儿。现金流量低。未来取决于Model S的成功。 
钱不是唯一的问题。马斯克正在处理质量控制问题，其中一些潜在危险。该公司将在已知容易泄漏的早期Model S中安装冷却系统。在此事的最新报道之后，美国国家公路交通安全管理局已经承认正在进行调查。有报道称由于球窝接头和控制臂破裂导致车轮脱落，中国政府最近下令对老式S型汽车进行大规模召回。特斯拉表示将遵守，但该公司将问题归咎于中国的驱动程序，而不是自己的产品。
 2014年4月，当Balan走进该安全办公室时，Model S项目的成功是特斯拉的当务之急。根据Balan的回忆，人事经理强烈建议她放弃对供应商合同的投诉。巴兰说不。 “好的，这是您的离职面试，” Balan回忆起被告知。她收到了辞呈，并要求他们签字。她说，当她抗议时，一名特斯拉官员扬言要把她的头戴上手铐带到外面，并告诉她：“如果你不知道怎么闭嘴，就会发生这种情况。”
 她说，巴兰感到被吓到了，想离开那里。即便如此，她仍然坚持在文件中添加一行：“我辞职是因为我一个月前被任命为卑诗省政府，我敢向高级管理人员说话，还有卑诗省那些有机会发表意见的人受到威胁...”她写道。保安人员护送她离开建筑物，进入停车场。
 但是特斯拉还没有解决她的问题。在她对公司提起仲裁要求后，《赫芬顿邮报》刊登了2017年有关她的案件的故事。运行后，特斯拉通讯高管戴夫·阿诺德（Dave Arnold）要求在线出版物全面刊登600字特斯拉的回应。它的部分内容为：“女士。 Balan花费公司时间在未经经理批准的情况下从事“秘密项目”的工作，并预订了未经批准的纽约旅行，但特斯拉为此花了一笔钱，为她自己创建的项目拜访了潜在的供应商。她还未经任何人的许可在特斯拉内部非法记录了内部谈话，这显然是犯罪行为。”
 阿诺德（Arnold）的文章没有提供任何证据来证实他的主张，《赫芬顿邮报》（Huffington Post）随后将故事记下了，尽管其存档版本仍然在线。
     Balan通过电子邮件和法庭上提交的其他文件表明，这个“秘密项目”根本不是秘密。根据电子邮件证据，正是挡风玻璃遮阳板项目吸引了特斯拉高层管理人员的支持，其中包括马斯克，她亲自向马斯克介绍了其背后的技术。
 当时的马斯克操作右手道格·道格（Doug Field）发表了几封有关该主题的电子邮件，其中一封信中写道：“这真是轻而易举；让我们花钱。对于[Model] X来说可能为时已晚，但这可能是未来车辆的一大特色。” （现任苹果公司高管的菲尔德对《泰晤士报》表示，他宁愿不讨论此案。） 
关于Balan的“未经批准”纽约之旅，时任特斯拉制造技术负责人，现在是Facebook的工程主管的Rich Heley向Balan发送了一封电子邮件，指出“您应该去纽约市”。 （Heley拒绝置评。）
     在特斯拉媒体部门的公开进攻未能阻止Balan之后，该公司的律师采取了另一种方法：将整个事情都进行仲裁和保密。
 美国的仲裁制度可以追溯到1925年，当时国会通过了《联邦仲裁法》。主要目的是通过将商业合同纠纷转移到另一个论坛来减轻法院系统的负担。自那以后，各种需要仲裁的案件如雨后春笋般涌现。到1980年代，坚持要求新员工签署仲裁协议作为雇用条件已成为标准惯例。
 亚历山大·J·S.2018年的一项研究经济政策研究所的科尔文估计，有6000万美国工人已经与其雇主签订了仲裁合同-而且这种合同在低薪工人中更为普遍。
  法学教授萨莱（Szalai）发现，美国100家最大的公司中有80％在工作场所纠纷中使用了仲裁，并说实际百分比几乎可以肯定更高。
 面对Balan声称自己遭到诽谤的指控，特斯拉的律师指出她的雇佣协议认为此事必须保留在私人仲裁中，而不是在公开法庭上。
 西雅图美国地方法院高级法官Marsha J.Pechman对此表示反对。在2019年6月，她发现，尽管特斯拉的某些指控与就业直接相关，必须继续进行仲裁，但其他指控可以在联邦法院进行审判，包括Balan是否因特斯拉的犯罪行为而受到诽谤。她指出，特斯拉没有提出支持这一指控的理由。 
法官还质疑特斯拉的法律立场：“您的立场是，如果从现在开始30年后，有一篇报纸文章表明原告发现她在某种程度上使她感到不适，那么无论她多大年龄，离她的工作有多远，那她必须仲裁吗？她已经永远签署了任何可能提及她先前工作的诉讼理由？”她说，根据笔录。
  特斯拉的律师没有直接回答，但向第九巡回法院提出上诉。口头辩论定于11月开始，但特斯拉律师赢得了延续至3月的辩论。律师说，他们正忙于其他审判。巴兰（Balan）认为他们想让她失望。
 她说，特斯拉针对她的主张的影响一直延续到今天。马斯克的一些极端支持者继续在推特上嘲笑她。
   尽管Balan案的细节很独特，但马斯克对批评家发起人身攻击的倾向却并非如此。马斯克追捕记者（包括本人），卖空者，举报人和内部批评家。
 时报一再征求有关各方的评论。为特斯拉（Tesla）诽谤案工作的三家律师事务所中没有一家会发表评论。特斯拉不再有一个公共关系部门，也不回应媒体的询问。 《泰晤士报》向投资者关系和几位公司高管发送了电子邮件，但没有回应。现任Facebook高管的戴夫·阿诺德（Dave Arnold）没有回复几封寻求评论的电子邮件。 《赫芬顿邮报》未回复置评请求。
  拥有大量资金的人可以用延误策略消灭同花顺的对手，这已经不是什么秘密了，因为他们用光了钱来付钱给自己的律师。这就是Balan拥有的净资产$ 50,000的原因，可以解释她为什么代表自己-她不必付律师的钱，所以时间是她唯一的支出。
 法官倾向于支持仲裁，因为它可以减轻巨大案件负担带来的压力，从而节省了系统的金钱和时间。 （最近，COVID-19大流行使加利福尼亚的民事诉讼几乎陷入停顿。）雇主之所以如此，Szalai说，这不仅是为了提高速度和便利性，还因为诉讼通常是保密的，而且新员工经常签订禁止他们加入集体诉讼的合同。 
他说，尽管偶尔会有改革的尝试，但是这使诸如性骚扰和种族歧视之类的问题无法看到。美国众议院于2019年通过了一项限制强制性仲裁的法案，但参议院从未通过。在特斯拉，股东于9月份拒绝了一项仲裁改革提案。
 某些尝试成功。在2018年员工要求罢工的罢工之后，谷歌于2019年终止了强制仲裁。
     Balan正在为上诉法庭的听证会做准备，该办公室位于她11岁儿子的卧室里，有一个临时办公室，她的HP电脑放在带褐色玻璃桌面的小木桌上。当她的儿子变得mb逼人时，她拿起iPad前往汽车。
 在诽谤诉讼中，她要求支付法律费用以及法院认为适当的任何惩罚性赔偿。她说，她欢迎任何金钱奖励，但是金钱不是她的动力。
 她说：“首先，我想清除我的名字。” “我想看到父母一生为我带来的骄傲。而且我想展示特斯拉想要摧毁某人时可以表现出的报仇程度。”
 第9巡回上诉法院将确定她是否能够在公共法庭上提起诉讼-也许将来还有很多像她这样的人可以自由这样做。
note_en:
The woman from human resources opened the doors — those big heavy fire doors with the push bars. She led the way through an unlighted, windowless room, draped in plastic, smelling of paint. Cristina Balan grew nervous. “This doesn’t look like the way to Elon’s office,” she thought to herself.
 She was led, she said, through another door into a room where she saw two large men in security uniforms behind desks. They were ordered to wait outside. Balan’s boss walked in. Balan was told to sit in a rolling desk chair. They were joined by a company lawyer.
  Weeks earlier, in a companywide email, Tesla Chief Executive Elon Musk had dumped on the concept of corporate hierarchy. Find “the fastest way to solve a problem to the benefit of the whole company” and don’t worry about your boss, he told them. Go straight to the top, if need be.
  Balan took him up on it. An automotive engineer at the electric car maker’s Fremont, Calif., assembly plant, she was motivated by Tesla’s save-the-planet mission and wanted it to succeed, she said.
 So she was bothered to see what she believed were contracts awarded based on friendships more than quality and price. She had also been raising concerns about floor mats installed in the then-new Model S that tended to curl up under the pedals, a potential safety hazard. Defective mats have caused crashes in other automakers’ vehicles.
  A few days later, the HR manager appeared at her desk and said, “So you want to talk to Elon, right? Well, let’s go.” But as soon as Balan saw the dark room with the plastic drapes, “I knew I should have returned to my desk,” she said. “This is weird, I thought. This is not going to be a meeting with Elon.”
    Instead, Balan said, she was forced to resign, an event that launched a six-year legal journey leading to where she is today: about to face off against Tesla — on her own, without an attorney — in the U.S. 9th Circuit Court of Appeals.
 Defamation is the main claim in Balan’s suit against Tesla — the company  accused her of criminal behavior on a popular online news site but provided no evidence to back up the charge. Balan says her professional reputation suffered damage so severe she can’t find a company willing to hire her. “They tell me, ‘We’d like to hire you, but we can’t afford to be on Musk’s blacklist,’” she said.
 More than 1,000 lawsuits have been filed against Tesla and Musk, a number of them alleging defamation, including the case in which Musk called a rescue diver who criticized him a “pedo” on Twitter and a “child rapist” in an email to a reporter without offering evidence. (A Los Angeles jury found the diver was not defamed.)
 But Balan’s appeals case goes beyond defamation. It raises questions about the limits of the legal practice known as mandatory arbitration, about whether an arbitration agreement can follow an employee for years or even decades after they’ve left a company — which is what Tesla lawyers argue as they attempt to keep the case out of court.
  Millions of employees in corporate America are asked to sign arbitration contracts, often with little knowledge of what they’re signing away. Given Tesla’s high profile, said Imre Szalai, an arbitration expert at the Loyola University New Orleans College of Law, “this will be a leading case on the topic of arbitration and defamation.”
   Cristina Balan grew up with grease under her fingernails in the Transylvania region of Romania. Other girls played with dolls. She took engines and motors apart and put them back together again. “I was fascinated with everything mechanical,” she said. “My toys were pliers and screwdrivers.”
 That set off some cluck-clucking among the neighbors. “I got a lot of badmouthing,” said her father, Ion Carstoc, then a mechanic at Romania’s national automotive research center and now retired. “I’ll never forget, one [neighbor] called me names, telling me to be a responsible parent. She said, ‘Cristina’s got oil on her clothes, she’s got oil in her hair. You need to teach her how to be a woman!’”
 Balan shrugged off the neighbors. After earning an engineering degree at Transylvania University of Braşov, she was hired by Boeing and traveled the world to work with suppliers for the 787 aircraft. In 2010, Tesla hired her to help design the battery pack for the now-iconic Model S all-electric luxury sedan.
  Based on evidence reviewed by The Times, Balan was well regarded by fellow workers and managers. Her performance reviews were glowing. She was known as a hard worker who put in long hours and was dedicated to creating high-quality products.
   Her initials are etched onto the battery pack clamshell cases she designed, which still can be found in older versions of the Model S. A performance review, entered in court, lauded her, saying, “She is often consulted by engineers from various departments when they come upon perplexing design and CAD challenges.” (CAD stands for computer-aided design.)
 A 2014 email from Daniel Ho, a top Tesla executive who now runs the Model 3 program, singled Balan out for praise. “Without creative engineers like you, this place would be just another car company,” he said. “DON’T LET THAT HAPPEN!” Ho did not respond to requests for comment.
     Balan’s problems began after she joined the interior fittings team. She developed a way to replace flip-down sun visors with touch-based windshield-dimming technology. Tesla never adopted it, but emails show the idea was reviewed by Musk, who urged that development be continued.
 All seemed to be going well until Balan came across what she considered sketchy dealings on supplier contracts and the curling floor mats. She said interior-engineering supervisors at the time acknowledged safety issues but noted that a recall on the floor mats would have been expensive.
 After she emailed her concerns to Musk, a Tesla lawyer took her aside, she said, and told her that if she continued to push matters, she should remember that some of her friends at Tesla had green card applications pending. (Contacted by The Times, the lawyer denied saying this.)
 Tesla was struggling for solvency at the time. It hadn’t yet become  a stock market darling. Cash flow was low. The future depended on the Model S’ success.
  Money wasn’t the only problem. Musk was dealing with quality-control issues, some potentially dangerous. The company was installing cooling systems in earlier versions of the Model S that it  knew were prone to leak. After recent stories on the matter, the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration has acknowledged it is conducting an investigation. Following reports that wheels fell off due to cracked ball joints and control arms, the Chinese government recently ordered a major recall of older Model S cars. Tesla says it will obey, but the company has  blamed China’s drivers for the problem, not its own products.
 The success of the Model S project was the top priority at Tesla in April 2014, when Balan was walked into that security office. According to Balan’s recollection, the HR manager strongly suggested she drop her complaints about the supplier contracts. Balan said no. “OK, this is your exit interview,” Balan recalls being told. She was handed resignation papers and asked to sign them. When she protested, she said, a Tesla official threatened to have her led outside in handcuffs and told her, “This is what happens if you don’t know how to keep your mouth shut.”
 Balan felt intimidated and wanted to get out of there, she said. Even so, she insisted on adding a line to the documents: “I’m resigning for the position that I was put in a month ago bc I dare to speak up to the Sr management, also bc people that had the chance to speak up were threatened...” she wrote. The security guards escorted her from the building and into the parking lot.
 But Tesla wasn’t through with her yet. After she filed an arbitration claim against the company, the Huffington Post ran a 2017 story about her case. After it ran, Tesla communications executive Dave Arnold demanded that the online publication run a 600-word Tesla response in full. In part, it read, “Ms. Balan spent company time working on a ‘secret project’ without her manager’s approval and booked an unapproved trip to New York at Tesla’s expense to visit a potential supplier for her own personally-created project. She also illegally recorded internal conversations within Tesla without anyone’s permission, which is clearly criminal conduct.”
 Arnold’s piece offered no evidence to substantiate his assertions, and the Huffington Post later took the story down, though an archived version  remains online.
     The “secret project,” Balan has shown through emails and other documents entered in court, was no secret at all. It was the windshield sun visor project that had attracted support from Tesla top executives, including Musk, whom she briefed personally about the technology behind it, according to emails entered into evidence.
 Doug Field, then Musk’s operational right hand man, issued several emails on the topic, including one that read, “This one is a no-brainer; let’s spend the money. It might be too late for [Model] X but this could be a big feature on future vehicles.” (Field, now an executive at Apple, told The Times he preferred not to discuss the case.)
 As to Balan’s “unapproved” trip to New York, Rich Heley — then head of manufacturing technology at Tesla and now an engineering executive at Facebook — sent Balan an email that stated “you should go to ny.” (Heley declined to comment.)
     After a public offensive by Tesla’s media department failed to deter Balan, the company’s lawyers took a different approach: Keep the whole thing in arbitration and under wraps.
 The arbitration system in the U.S. goes back to 1925, when Congress passed the Federal Arbitration Act. The main purpose was to relieve the burden on the court system by moving business contract disputes into another forum. The variety of cases subject to arbitration has mushroomed since then. By the 1980s, the insistence that new hires sign arbitration agreements as a condition of employment became standard practice.
 A 2018 study by Alexander J.S. Colvin for the Economic Policy Institute estimates that 60 million American workers have  signed arbitration contracts with their employers — and that such contracts are more common for low-wage workers.
  Law professor Szalai found that  80% of the 100 largest U.S. companies have used arbitration in workplace disputes, and said the actual percentage was almost certainly higher.
 Presented with Balan’s claim that she had been defamed, Tesla’s lawyers pointed to her employment agreement to argue  the matter must remain in private arbitration and not in public court.
 Marsha J. Pechman, senior judge of the U.S. District Court in Seattle, disagreed. In June 2019, she found that although some of Tesla’s allegations were directly related to employment and must remain in arbitration, others could be tried in federal court, including whether Balan was defamed by Tesla’s assertion of criminality. She noted that Tesla had not made a case to back up the charge.
 The judge also questioned Tesla’s legal stance: “It’s your position that, if 30 years from now there is a newspaper article that the plaintiff finds somehow maligns her, that no matter how old she is, no matter how far away her employment was from this, that she has to arbitrate it? She has forever signed away any cause of action that might mention her prior employment?” she said, according to a transcript.
  Tesla lawyers had no direct answer but filed an appeal to the 9th Circuit. Oral arguments were set to begin in November, but Tesla lawyers won a continuance to March. The lawyers said they were busy with other trials. Balan thinks they’re trying to wear her down.
 The effect of Tesla’s claims against her follow her to this day, she said. She continues to be  pilloried on Twitter by some of Musk’s more  extreme supporters.
   Although the details in Balan’s case are unique, Musk’s proclivity to  launch personal attacks on critics is not. Musk has gone after  reporters (including this one),  short sellers,  whistleblowers and  internal critics.
 The Times repeatedly sought comment from all parties involved. None of the three law firms that have worked for Tesla on the defamation case would comment. Tesla no longer has a public relations department and does not respond to media inquiries; The Times sent emails to investor relations and several corporate executives, without response. Dave Arnold, now an executive at Facebook, did not respond to several emails seeking comment. The Huffington Post did not respond to a request for comment.
  It’s no secret that those with plenty of money can use delay tactics to wear down less-flush opponents, as they run out of money to pay their own lawyers. That’s a reason Balan, with a stated net worth of $50,000, gives to explain why she is representing herself — she doesn’t have to pay lawyers, so time is her only expense.
 Judges tend to support arbitration because it relieves pressure from enormous caseloads, saving the system money and time. (More recently, the COVID-19 pandemic has brought civil proceedings in California to a near-standstill.) Employers like it, Szalai said, not just for speed and convenience, but also because the proceedings are often kept secret, and new hires often sign contracts that ban them from joining class-action lawsuits.
 That keeps issues such as sexual harassment and racial discrimination hidden from view, he said, though there are occasional attempts at reform. A bill to limit mandatory arbitration was passed by the U.S. House of Representatives in 2019 but was never taken up by the Senate. At Tesla, shareholders rejected an arbitration reform proposal in September.
 Some attempts are successful. In 2019 Google ended mandatory arbitration after  a 2018 walkout by employees demanding it be abolished.
     Balan is preparing for the appeals court hearing from a makeshift office in her 11-year-old son’s bedroom, her HP computer on a small wooden desk with a brown glass top. When her son gets rambunctious, she takes her iPad and moves to her car.
 In her defamation suit, she’s asking for legal expenses and whatever punitive damages the court sees fit. She said she’d welcome any monetary awards, but money’s not her motivation.
 “First of all, I want to clear my name,” she said. “I want to see the pride in my parents’ eyes that they’ve had for me all my life. And I want to show the level of vindictiveness Tesla can go to when they want to destroy somebody.”
 The 9th Circuit Court of Appeals will determine whether she’s able to make her case in a public courtroom — and perhaps whether many others like her will be free to do so in the future.
title: 特斯拉称她为罪犯。 她的战斗可能是员工权益的里程碑
title_en: Tesla called her a criminal. Her fight could be a milestone for employee rights

此次收购对于MTG来说是重要的一笔交易，因为该公司将立即花费2.75亿美元，并再拨出1亿美元用于基于绩效的支付。
 如果您不熟悉MTG，则可能知道其投资组合公司。在过去的几年中，MTG收购了ESL和DreamHack成为电子竞技的领导者。
  MTG还因其流行的基于网络和手机游戏而收购了InnoGames和Kongregate。听起来MTG尚未完成，因为该公司计划在不久的将来收购更多公司。
 MTG在公告中解释了此次收购为何有意义的原因。 Hutch Games并不专注于单一游戏。它建立了一系列成功的游戏，这始终是未来增长的好兆头。
 游戏工作室还许可了一些知名品牌，例如F1。 MTG相信F1经理，Top Drives和Rebel Racing仍具有很大的增长潜力。免费手机游戏已成为一种游戏即服务，因此从长远来看，您希望使其流行。
 就长期潜力而言，Hutch Games还将在2021年和2022年推出更多游戏。最后，由于大多数员工都是开发人员，因此这是一个经济高效的工作室。
 在2020年的前9个月中，Hutch Games的收入为5,630万美元，息税前利润（EBIT）为1,330万美元。 Hutch Games的投资者包括Backed VC，Index Ventures，Initial Capital和天使投资人Chris Lee。 
如您所见，免费游戏可以赚钱。 因此，将来会有一些合并。 有些公司会动用自己的财大气粗或利用债务来建立庞大的投资组合和主屏幕的资产，一次只玩一次。
note_en:
The acquisition is an important one for  MTG   as the company is spending $275 million right away and setting aside another $100 million for performance-based payments.
 If you’re not familiar with MTG, you probably know its portfolio companies. Over the past few years, MTG has acquired ESL and DreamHack to become an esports leader.
  MTG has also acquired InnoGames and Kongregate for their popular web-based and mobile games. And it sounds like MTG isn’t done just yet as the company plans to acquire more companies in the near future.
 MTG explains why the acquisition makes sense in its  announcement. Hutch Games isn’t focused on a single game. It has built a portfolio of successful games, which is always a good sign for future growth.
 The game studio has also licensed some well-known brands, such as F1. And MTG believes that F1 Manager, Top Drives and Rebel Racing still have a lot of growth potential. Free-to-play mobile games have become games as a service, so you want to keep them popular over the long run.
 When it comes to long-term potential,  Hutch   Games also has more games in the pipeline for 2021 and 2022. Finally, it’s a cost-effective studio as most employees are developers.
 During the first nine months of 2020, Hutch Games generated $56.3 million in revenue and $13.3 million in earnings before interest and taxes (EBIT). Hutch Games investors included Backed VC, Index Ventures, Initial Capital and angel investor Chris Lee.
 As you can see, free-to-play games can be lucrative. That’s why there will be some consolidation in the future. Some companies will use their deep pockets or take advantage of debt to build out big portfolios and the real estate of your home screen, one game at a time.
title: MTG斥资3.75亿美元收购移动赛车游戏工作室Hutch Games

新任首席执行官带来了30年的行业经验，包括在Broadcom，Cisco，Nortel和IBM的工作经历。此外，他最近在VMware担任VMware产品和云服务首席运营官一职。
 Ramaswami在VMware的职位上有机会将Nutanix视为主要竞争对手，现在他有机会带领公司进入下一阶段。他在一份声明中说：“长期以来，我一直很欣赏Nutanix是强大的竞争对手，超融合基础架构解决方案的先驱和云软件的领导者。”他希望利用自己的行业知识来继续发展公司。
   Nutanix的首席独立董事Sohaib Abbasi说，作为候选人，拉马斯瓦米的经历确实很突出。他在声明中说：“拉吉夫以罕见的操作纪律，业务敏锐度，技术远见和包容性的领导才能而在首席执行官候选人中脱颖而出。”
 Constellation Research分析师Holger Mueller表示，由于VMware正在迅速成为该公司的主要竞争对手，因此聘用具有很大的意义。他告诉我：“ Nutanix和VMware希望将来保持一致-瑞士的虚拟化和工作负载可移植性跨云和内部计算基础架构。”
 更重要的是，它使Nutanix可以吸引有才能的高管。他说：“因此，雇用Ramaswami既将多云专家带入Nutanix掌舵，又从人才的角度削弱了关键竞争对手。”
 Nutanix成立于2009年。根据Crunchbase的数据，该公司从Khosla Ventures，Lightspeed Ventures，Sapphire Ventures，Fidelity和Wellington Management等公司筹集了超过6亿美元的资金。该公司于2016年上市。投资者对该公告的发布感到高兴，该公司股价在发布时上涨了1.29％。
note_en:
The new CEO brings 30 years of industry experience to the position including stints with Broadcom, Cisco, Nortel and IBM — in addition to his most recent gig at VMware as Chief Operating Officer of Products and Cloud Services at VMware.
 At his position at VMware, Ramaswami had the opportunity to see  Nutanix   up close as a key competitor, and he now has the opportunity to lead the company into its next phase. “I have long admired Nutanix as a formidable competitor, a pioneer in hyperconverged infrastructure solutions and a leader in cloud software,” he said in a statement. He hopes to build on his industry knowledge to continue growing the company.
   Sohaib Abbasi, lead independent director of Nutanix says that as a candidate, Ramaswami’s experience really stood out. “Rajiv distinguished himself among the CEO candidates with his rare combination of operational discipline, business acumen, technology vision and inclusive leadership skills,” he said in statement.
 Holger Mueller, an analyst at Constellation Research says the hiring makes a lot of sense as VMware is quickly becoming the company’s primary competitor. “Nutanix and VMware want to be the same in the future — the virtualization and workload portability Switzerland across cloud and on premise compute infrastructures,” he told me.
 What’s more, it allows Nutanix to grab a talented executive. “So hiring Ramaswami brings both an expert for multi-cloud to the Nutanix helm, as well as weakening a key competitor from a talent perspective,” he said.
 Nutanix was founded in 2009. It raised over $600 million from firms like Khosla Ventures, Lightspeed Ventures, Sapphire Ventures, Fidelity and Wellington Management, according to Crunchbase data. The  company went public in 2016. Investors seem pleased by the announcement with the company stock price up 1.29% as of publication.
title: Nutanix聘请前VMware高管为新首席执行官
title_en: Nutanix brings in former VMware exec as new CEO

苹果供应商责任团队的三位前成员表示，苹果在中国违反劳动法。他们得到了熟悉苹果公司中国业务的前苹果高级经理的支持。
 他们说，苹果公司意识到其供应商的违规行为，但未采取任何行动，因为它担心这样做可能会延迟产品的发布……
  该信息说，2014年颁布了一项新法律，规定临时合同雇用的雇员不得超过10％。这是因为临时工的工作不稳定，并且往往获得的福利较少。
 该法律规定了为期两年的宽限期，苹果公司通过调查有多少供应商在该法律生效后会违反该法律而开始工作。
 苹果当年对中国的362家供应商工厂进行了调查，发现其中近一半超出了临时工的配额。根据The Information审阅的苹果内部演示，有80家工厂雇用了临时工人，占其劳动力的一半以上。苹果要求供应商提出计划，在法律的两年宽限期到期之前，在2016年3月的截止日期之前减少对临时工的使用。
 但是，据报道，在该法律生效之时，进展甚微，前雇员说苹果决定不理会它，而不是对供应商违反法律采取行动。
 根据四名熟悉劳资问题的前苹果雇员的说法，苹果多年来一直没有对供应商采取任何重大行动，因为担心这会增加成本，浪费资源并推迟产品发布，从而违反了临时工劳动法。前苹果员工中有三位是其供应商责任团队的成员，该团队负责监控违规行为并执行处罚，而第四位是熟悉其在中国业务的高级经理。 
如果属实，这对于苹果公司来说尤其令人尴尬，因为苹果公司声称对自己的供应链采取负责任的态度，以确保工作条件既合法又合理。
  去年苹果再次承认富士康在其郑州庞大的iPhone工厂违反了法律，该工厂可雇用多达30万名工人，这一问题再次在公开场合浮出水面。苹果表示，它要求供应商遵守当地法律，并承诺删除不符合要求的产品。
 最近和硕也发生了类似的事情，当时发现和硕违反了苹果的供应商代码，而且据报道，苹果因强迫劳动而放弃了其iPhone相机供应商之一。该公司还游说要求改变会影响美国公司的强迫劳动法案。
 但文章说，根本问题是苹果公司的产品保密方法。为了最大程度地减少泄漏时间，该公司希望将生产推迟到尽可能接近启动日期的时间。前员工说，这实际上使供应商无法遵守法律，而苹果公司对此很清楚。
 内部数据显示，苹果高管知道其生产策略导致临时工需求增加。苹果在2015年的一次内部演示中说：“我们的“惊喜和喜悦”业务模型在我们推出产品时仅需要很短的时间就需要大量的劳动力。我们正在使供应商难以遵守这一要求。 10％派遣的法律根本不足以应付我们在升级期间所需的劳动力需求激增。”
 苹果公司回应说，它与供应商“密切合作”以纠正违法行为。
 工作场所的权利是人权，我们的供应商行为准则是​​业内最强的，并且同样适用于我们供应链中的每个人。有时工厂使用临时工，我们会对此进行密切监控以确保符合我们的规范。如果发现问题，我们将与供应商紧密合作，制定纠正措施计划。

note_en:
Three former members of the  Apple Supplier Responsibility team say Apple was complicit in labor law violations in China. They are supported by a former Apple senior manager familiar with the company’s Chinese operations.
 They say Apple was aware of the violations by its suppliers, but took no action because it feared that to do so might delay product launches …
  The Information says that a new law was introduced in 2014, stipulating that no more than 10% of employees be hired on temporary contracts. This is because temporary workers have more precarious employment, and often receive fewer benefits.
 The law allowed a two-year grace period for compliance, and Apple started out well by investigating how many suppliers would be in breach of the law when it took effect.
 Apple surveyed 362 of its supplier factories in China that year and discovered that nearly half were over the quota for temporary workers. Eighty factories used temporary workers for more than half their labor force, according to an internal Apple presentation reviewed by The Information. Apple asked its suppliers to come up with plans to reduce their use of temporary workers by a March 2016 deadline, when a two-year grace period for the law expired.
 However, little progress was reportedly made by the time the law came into force, and rather than take action against suppliers breaking the law, the former employees say that Apple decided to ignore it.
 According to four former Apple employees familiar with its labor issues, Apple for years took no major action against its suppliers for violating the temp-worker labor law out of concerns it would create costs, drain resources and delay product launches. Three of the ex-Apple employees were members of its supplier responsibility team, which is in charge of monitoring violations and enforcing penalties, while the fourth was a senior manager familiar with its operations in China.
 If true, it’s particularly embarrassing for Apple, which claims to have a responsible approach to its supply chain, ensuring that working conditions are both legal and reasonable.
  The issue surfaced again publicly last year when Apple admitted that Foxconn had broken the law at its massive iPhone factory in Zhengzhou, which can employ as many as 300,000 workers. Apple says it requires suppliers to abide by local laws and pledges to remove those that won’t comply.
 A similar thing  happened recently with Pegatron, when the company was found to have breached Apple’s supplier code, and Apple has also been reported to have dropped one of its iPhone camera suppliers over  the use of forced labor. The company has also  lobbied for changes to a forced labor bill that would impact American companies.
 But the piece says that the underlying issue is Apple’s approach to product secrecy. To minimize the time for leaks, the company wants production to be delayed until as close as possible to the launch date. This makes it virtually impossible for suppliers to comply with the law, say the former staff – and Apple was well aware of this.
 Apple executives knew that its production strategy caused the increase in demand for temporary workers, internal data shows. “Our ‘surprise and delight’ business model requires a huge volume of labor for only a short period of time as we ramp products,” according to an internal Apple presentation in 2015. “We are making it difficult for our suppliers to comply with this law as 10% dispatch is simply not enough to cope with the spikes in labor demand we require during our ramps.”
 Apple said in response that it ‘works closely’ with suppliers to correct breaches of the law.
 Workplace rights are human rights and our supplier code of conduct is the strongest in the industry, and it applies equally to everyone across our supply chain. Occasionally factories use temporary labor, and we monitor this closely to ensure compliance with our code. Where we find issues we work closely with the supplier on corrective action plans.
  You’re reading 9to5Mac — experts who break news about Apple and its surrounding ecosystem, day after day. Be sure to check out  our homepage for all the latest news, and follow 9to5Mac on  Twitter,  Facebook, and  LinkedIn to stay in the loop. Don’t know where to start?
